# Open Data {#sec-OAdata}

## Why open data matters

* *Brief motivation:* reproducibility, re-use, equity. 
* Note music research's particular challenges (audio licensing, participant privacy, sparse sharing norms) — hook to @eerola2023transparency and @moss2021fair.

## Data management

* Working open data into your research workflow 
  * (addressing the extra workload many feel it puts on the backend of their research)
  * Planning to share from the start/conceptualisation means less remaking/polishing at the end

### Data management plans

* Funder requirements (UKRI, Horizon Europe, NIH). 
* What a DMP covers. 
* Practical tools (DMPonline, OSF). 
* When to write one.

## FAIR principles in music research

* Findable / Accessible / Interoperable / Reusable [@moss2021fair].
* Music-specific metadata standards (MusicXML, MEI, JAMS, MIREX conventions). 
* Persistent identifiers (DOIs, ORCIDs for datasets).

> A table of common music data types (audio, symbolic, behavioural, physiological, text) with FAIR considerations per type.

## Repositories and archiving

* Zenodo, OSF, Dryad, domain-specific (ISMIR datasets, music21 corpus). 
* Embargo options. 
* Versioning (with care not to overlap too much with Reprodicible section). 
* Long-term preservation.

## Sharing quantitative and computational data

* Formats (CSV, JSON, HDF5, MIDI, MusicXML). 
* Codebooks and READMEs. 
* Code alongside data (links to Ch6). 

> Worked example from a corpus study or MIR dataset.

## Qualitative and sensitive data

* What "open" means when data are participant descriptions, interviews, or think-aloud protocols. 
* Consent and GDPR constraints. 
* Anonymisation strategies (transcripts, demographic data). 
* Partial openness: sharing codebooks, coding schemes, or aggregated outputs even when raw data cannot be shared. 
* Qualitative Archive options (UK Data Service / QUALIDATA, DANS).

## Original materials, stimuli, and copyright issues

* Audio stimuli in psychology experiments — licensing, Creative Commons, public domain options. 
* Commercially licensed music: what can/can't be shared and workarounds (stimulus lists + timestamps, Freesound, commissioned recordings). 
  * need careful handling given the gap between what researchers *do* with commercial recordings and what's technically shareable.
* Copyright for notated scores. 
* Synthetic stimuli and why they're an open science advantage.

## Summary

* Gradient of openness — some data sharing is always better than none. 
* Pointers to Ch6 (open code) and Ch9 (preregistration of data collection plans).
